title: Loss-induced quantum nonreciprocity and entanglement in superconducting qubits
abstract: Losses are ubiquitous in physics and are usually regarded as harmful in quantum information processing. Here, we propose a loss-induced scheme to achieve nonreciprocity and nonreciprocal entanglement in a superconducting platform, where two remote superconducting transmon qubits are connected via two lossy auxiliary cavities. The nonreciprocity in our scheme originates from interference between multiple lossy coupling paths. The coherent phases associated with the qubit-resonator couplings reverse sign under propagation reversal, while the loss-induced phases remain direction independent. Their combined effect leads to different interference conditions in the opposite directions, resulting in unequal effective couplings. We show that this loss-induced scheme can generate nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, indicating that loss can be utilized as a resource. Moreover, the tunability of nonreciprocity and nonreciprocal entanglement in our scheme can be manipulated by the relative phase induced by loss, allowing to tailor both reciprocal and nonreciprocal behaviors. Our results establish a direct link between engineered loss and nonreciprocal entanglement in quantum information processing and offer potential applications in scalable quantum networks.
summary: This paper proposes a method to achieve unidirectional (nonreciprocal) interaction and entanglement between superconducting qubits using loss as a resource. By engineering interference between multiple lossy paths, the authors show that one can create an isolator-like effect without needing bulky magnetic fields. This approach is scalable and robust against typical experimental noise like dephasing and detuning.
main_problem: Developing magnetic-free, scalable, and on-chip nonreciprocal devices (isolators/circulators) for superconducting quantum platforms while exploring if loss can be used as a resource for entanglement.
main_result: The authors propose a scheme where loss-induced phase interference in a multi-channel system achieves nonrecipropectivity and nonreciprocal entanglement, demonstrating that loss can be utilized as a resource for quantum networks.
method: The study uses adiabatic elimination of lossy modes, the Heisenberg-Langevin formalism, and a two-tone flux-modulation protocol to derive an effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: A superconducting circuit QED architecture consisting of two remote transmon qubits connected via two lossy auxiliary cavities/waveguides, modeled using a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Nonreciprocity (isolation factor), nonreciprocal entanglement (concurrence), and qubit population dynamics.
important_parameters: Isolation factor (delta h), coherent phase (phi), loss-induced phase (theta), and asymmetric detuning configurations.
main_assumptions: The validity of the effective Hamiltonian relies on the slow-timescale approximation and the ability to average out high-frequency oscillations induced by drives.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the schematic and physical implementation of the engineered system; Fig 2/3 illustrate population dynamics and the transition between reciprocal and unidirectional regimes; Fig 4/5 demonstrate the robustness of the scheme against decoherence and detuning; Fig 7 validates the effective model against the full time-dependent model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonreciprocity, presents the engineered multi-channel model, derives the effective Hamiltonian via adiabatic elimination, demonstrates the generation of nonreciprocal entanglement, tests the robustness against experimental imperfections, and validates the implementation via flux modulation.
why_interesting: It provides a novel perspective on non-Hermitian physics by demonstrating how engineered dissipation and loss-induced phases can break time-reversal symmetry to create unidirectional quantum gates and entanglement.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the engineered system model. A pair of two qubits QL and QR are connected by a series of connecting modes ˆc(n), with the engineered complex couplings g(n) L/R. These engineered couplings are generated by applying parametric modulations to the qubits in the presence of the bare couplings. (b) Circuit implementation of two transmon qubits (pink) QL and QR connected by two auxiliary cavities (blue) via capacitive couplings. (c) Schematic diagram of the realization of nonreciprocity between qubits through the interfere between two channels.
